name: Pallet Load (cube method)
description: Theoretical cases per pallet from the cube method: the usable pallet volume divided by the volume of one case. Warehouse-planning texts use this ratio to size storage cube before applying layout allowances.

inputs.name: Case length
inputs.description: Outer case length; must be greater than zero.

inputs.name: Case width
inputs.description: Outer case width; must be greater than zero.

inputs.name: Case height
inputs.description: Outer case height; must be greater than zero.

outputs.name: Cube-method cases
outputs.description: Theoretical cases per pallet by pure volume ratio — an upper bound, not a load plan.

assumptions: Volume is perfectly divisible — every cubic inch of pallet cube is usable.
limitations: A theoretical upper bound: real stacking geometry (whole cases, orientations, patterns) fits fewer cases — compare freight.pallet.layer_fit for the conservative estimate.
Ignores stacking geometry, weight limits, crush strength, and stability.
Warehouse planning applies further allowances (aisles, honeycombing) on top of this ratio.

examples.note: 115,200 in3 of usable pallet cube / 1,920 in3 per case = 60 cases by pure volume — versus 54 by whole-case layer fit.

citations.quote: Cubic feet of one pallet of item: 2 ft wide - 2 ft long - 6 (3 levels) ft high = 96 ft3 ... Cases per pallet: 96 ft3 / 8 ft3 (per case) = 12 cases per pallet
Cube utilization. All stocking equipment should be filled to the maximum cube capacity ... Allowances for aisles and honeycombing must be taken into consideration when planning warehouse layouts.
